narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which stands above the typical student's 3.21, and a solid LSAT score of 42 that exceeds the average of 36.70. Your Year 1 school decile of 8 is also stronger than the average of 5.71, reflecting early academic promise, while your upper-middle family income provides a supportive backdrop shared by fewer applicants. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 62.6% chance you'll face failure—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your undergraduate GPA; even though it's above average, it had the strongest negative pull in your specific case, outweighing its typical positive signal. Next, your Year 3 school decile of 9—higher than the average 5.53—worked strongly in your favor, suggesting a boost toward passing that partially offset concerns. Your upper-middle family income then contributed toward the failure prediction, as it sometimes correlates with other pressures in these models. Your Year 1 decile added a smaller push toward failure, while your LSAT and full-time status had minimal sway. Compared to typical students, your profile shines in several areas but was edged out by how the model weighed your GPA and later-year performance against these averages. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—it's one data-driven view. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT for an even higher score, strengthening recent grades if possible, or highlighting bar prep experience in a new application to shift future predictions. We're rooting for you to rewrite this story.
